reasoning:
The market is pricing this escalation risk at 35% as of October 30, 2025, with ~5 months left until the resolution cutoff (March 31, 2026). However, both NATO and Russia have maintained extreme restraint regarding the direct, kinetic use of force against each other since the start of the Ukraine war in early 2022, recognizing it as a severe red line. The strict resolution criteria explicitly rule out the most common forms of "spillover" friction: interceptions of stray one-way munitions (like Shahed drones or errant cruise missiles) do not count, and physical collisions involving drones (such as the 2023 Black Sea Reaper incident) or warning shots also do not qualify. To resolve "Yes", there must be an explicit live-fire engagement between forces or the shooting down of a non-munitions unit like a reconnaissance drone or manned aircraft.

Prediction markets like Polymarket heavily skew toward overpricing geopolitical tail risks, often carrying a 10-20% premium for sensational conflict scenarios due to favorite-longshot and headline biases. While elevated geopolitical tension likely props the price up at 35%, the actual base rate of direct kinetic NATO-Russia encounters over the past 44 months of the war is zero. Expecting one in the next 5 months purely through technical escalation is unlikely, especially when both actors rely heavily on "gray zone" and non-kinetic tactics. Thus, adjusting downward to ~22% respects any recent hidden signals in the market while correcting for historical base rates, stringent criteria, and established market biases.
